text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Menon  
St George’s University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Priya Menon,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-03-1988, Hospital No: H392847 NHS No. 9081736452  
21 Brookfield Avenue, Wandsworth, London SW18 4JP 

Diagnoses: 

History of recurrent seizures; MRI (2019) showed left mesial temporal scarring; EEG (2020) intermittent left temporal sharp transients without clear generalised features. 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily  
Vitamin D3 1000 IU once daily 

Plan: 

- Continue current anti-seizure therapy and safety precautions.  
- Routine bloods (U&Es, FBC, LFTs) via GP in 6 months to monitor general health while on therapy.  
- Maintain sleep hygiene and limit alcohol; patient to continue structured seizure diary and peer group check-ins.  
- DVLA advice reiterated: to notify if any further events occur.  
- Open appointment: earlier review if breakthrough events, adverse effects, or treatment concerns. 

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports good overall wellbeing and tolerates levetiracetam without mood changes, irritability or somatic adverse effects. He continues to attend a local peer support group; group members have not observed any episodes suggestive of seizures during shared activities and their observations supplement his history. With structural focal epilepsy presenting as focal non-motor and secondarily generalised convulsions, he has been seizure free for a long duration and has not reported seizures for over one years. He keeps a monthly digital diary and his partner corroborates the absence of nocturnal events, tongue biting, unprovoked injuries, or witnessed episodes. There have been no recent head injuries, intercurrent illnesses, or medication changes. 

His last brain MRI (2019) demonstrated left mesial temporal scarring consistent with prior hippocampal injury; no interval imaging has been required as there are no red flags or clinical deterioration. The prior EEG (2020) showed intermittent left temporal sharp transients without evolution to generalised discharges. Routine blood tests from his GP in March 2025 were within normal ranges. 

We discussed that sustained seizure freedom reduces but does not eliminate future risk. He prefers to remain on his current dose at present, particularly as it supports his confidence at work and while travelling. We agreed to continue current therapy, reinforce general lifestyle measures (regular sleep, hydration, avoidance of binge alcohol), and use the peer group’s structured check-ins to supplement monitoring. I advised prompt contact if any suggestive symptoms recur (auras, blank spells, witnessed convulsions) or if he experiences medication side effects. 

I have arranged an open follow-up and will be happy to review sooner if needed. 

With best wishes,

entities.text: he has been seizure free for a long duration and has not reported seizures for over one years
With structural focal epilepsy presenting as focal non-motor and secondarily generalised convulsions, he has been seizure free for a long duration and has not reported seizures for over one years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient has been seizure free for a long duration and has not reported seizures for over one year. The clinic date is 02 October 2025, and there is no indication of any seizures within the past year. This meets the criterion of being seizure free for at least 6 months, so the correct normalized label is 'seizure free for 1 year' or potentially more; however, the explicit numeric expression provided is 'over one years'. Normalizing to allowed formats, the closest precise label is 'seizure free for 1 year' since 'over one year' should be rounded to the nearest explicit numeric period stated.
